section.title: Gaius Commended for Hospitality
section.sectionRationale: The letter turns from praise of Gaius the man to praise of a particular thing he does: he welcomes and supports travelling gospel workers who went out for the Name and took nothing from outsiders. This movement gives the letter its practical heart, showing what walking in the truth looks like in action.
section.thesis: Hospitality to gospel workers is not a minor courtesy but partnership in the truth: by supporting those who go out for the Name, Gaius becomes a fellow worker in their mission.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Gaius's faithful welcome of strangers; sending them on worthily because they went out for the Name; and the call to support such workers as fellow workers for the truth.

verses.text: Beloved, you are {{pistos|faithful}} in what you are doing for the brothers, and especially since they are {{xenos|strangers}} to you.
They have testified to the church about your love. You will do well to send them on their way in a manner {{axios|worthy}} of God.

units.label: Partners in the mission
units.note: for the Name they went out

verses.text: For they went out on behalf of the {{onoma|Name}}, accepting nothing from the Gentiles.
Therefore we ought to support such men, so that we may be {{synergos|fellow workers}} for the truth.

pistos.gloss: Faithful, trustworthy, reliable. It describes someone who can be counted on to do what is right, again and again. Gaius is commended not for a single grand act but for a faithful pattern of caring for the brothers, especially those he had never met.

xenos.gloss: A stranger or foreigner; the root behind the New Testament word for hospitality, which literally means love of strangers. The point is striking: Gaius served brothers who were strangers to him, welcoming them for the sake of Christ rather than because of prior friendship.

axios.gloss: In a manner worthy of, fitting to. To send the workers on their way worthy of God means to provide for them generously and honourably, as a right reflection of the God they serve, not with grudging or minimal help.

onoma.gloss: Name. On behalf of the Name means for the sake of Jesus; the Name stands for His person and honour. The workers travelled for Christ's sake and refused pagan support, so the church must be the ones to sustain them.

synergos.gloss: A fellow worker, co-labourer. By supporting the travelling teachers, those who stay home become genuine partners in the same mission. Hospitality and giving are not spectator kindness; they make the giver a true colleague in the work of the truth.

basic.context: Now the elder praises something specific: Gaius has been opening his home to travelling gospel workers, even ones he had never met. These men went out to preach for Jesus and refused to take money from unbelievers, so it fell to fellow Christians to support them. The elder says that when Gaius helps them, he becomes a partner in their mission.
basic.hermeneutics: This is warm, practical instruction, not a rigid rule about house guests. The abiding principle is clear and still binding: God's people should generously support those who give their lives to the gospel, and by doing so they share in the work itself. Hospitality here is mission, not just manners.
basic.originalLanguages: Gaius is faithful, pistos, reliable over time. The men are strangers, xenos, the very word behind hospitality, which means love of strangers. He is to send them on worthy, axios, of God. They went out for the Name, onoma, that is, for Jesus. And those who support them become synergos, fellow workers, in the truth.
basic.typology: The travelling workers who go out for the Name, taking nothing from outsiders, follow the pattern Jesus set when He sent out His disciples to depend on the welcome of God's people. Gaius's open home is a small picture of the whole church as a place of welcome for Christ's messengers.
basic.christ: The workers went out for the Name, the name of Jesus. Christ Himself had nowhere to lay His head and sent His followers to rely on hospitality. To welcome His messengers is, in His own words, to welcome Him. Supporting the mission is a way of serving Christ directly.
basic.god: God is honoured when His people care generously for gospel workers, sending them on their way in a manner worthy of God Himself. He notices faithful, quiet service to strangers and counts it as partnership in His mission.
basic.crossReferences: Jesus said welcoming His messengers is welcoming Him. Paul teaches that those who preach the gospel should live by the gospel. And Hebrews urges believers not to neglect hospitality to strangers.
basic.oneStory: God's plan to reach the nations runs through sent workers and the people who support them. Gaius's open door is a thread in that great story: ordinary believers making the mission possible, becoming fellow workers for the truth that is spreading to the world.

context.literary: The commendation section, moving from Gaius's character to a specific commended action. It builds an argument: Gaius is faithful, verse 5; the workers deserve worthy support, verse 6; because they went out for the Name and refused pagan money, verse 7; therefore the church must support them and so share the work, verse 8. This positive model stands in deliberate contrast to Diotrephes in the verses that follow.
context.historical: Travelling teachers and evangelists moved between the young churches, carrying the gospel and letters. Inns were scarce, costly, and often disreputable, so hospitality in believers' homes was vital. These workers refused support from Gentiles, unbelievers, so as not to compromise the gospel or appear to peddle it, which made the support of fellow Christians essential to the mission.
context.canonical: A concrete example of the New Testament ethic of hospitality and gospel partnership, echoing Jesus' sending of the disciples and Paul's teaching on supporting gospel workers. It shows the early church's mission depending on a web of welcome and generosity.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To commend Gaius for his faithful hospitality to gospel workers, to explain why such support is right and necessary, and to call the wider church to be fellow workers for the truth by sustaining those sent out for the Name.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes Gaius's example but also prescribes: we ought to support such men. The enduring command is generous support of genuine gospel workers; the specific form, lodging travelling teachers, is the first-century shape of a principle that still binds.

debates.question: Why did the workers accept nothing from the Gentiles?

views.label: To keep the gospel free of charge
views.case: Refusing pagan money kept the message from looking like a paid service or being under obligation to unbelievers, so the good news could be offered freely and credibly.

views.label: To place the duty on the church
views.case: By not taking outside support, the workers made clear that sustaining the mission was the responsibility and privilege of God's people, which is exactly the elder's point in verse 8.

debates.bearing: Both readings lead to the same call: because the workers would not lean on outsiders, believers must support them, and in doing so become partners in the truth.

study.originalLanguages: The commendation turns on a cluster of vivid words. Gaius is pistos, faithful, dependable in a settled pattern of care. Those he serves are xenoi, strangers, the very root of the word hospitality, which means love of strangers, so his welcome is love reaching beyond the circle of friends. He is to send them on axios, worthy, of God, generously and honourably. They went out for the onoma, the Name, for Jesus' sake, taking nothing from unbelievers. And so, the elder concludes, those who support them become synergoi, fellow workers, genuine colleagues in the work of the truth.

typology.type: Workers sent out for the Name, depending on welcome
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the pattern of Christ sending the seventy-two to rely on the hospitality of God's people, Luke 10, and in the ongoing sending of the church.
A picture of the whole church as a household of welcome for Christ's messengers, and ultimately of the Father's house that welcomes the redeemed.

study.christ: The workers went out for the Name, and that Name is Jesus. Christ, who had nowhere to lay His head, sent His disciples to depend on the welcome of others, and He taught that whoever receives them receives Him, and whoever receives Him receives the Father. So Gaius's hospitality is, in the deepest sense, service rendered to Christ. Supporting the mission of the gospel is one of the most direct ways ordinary believers labour alongside their Lord.
study.god: God is honoured by generous, faithful care for those who serve Him; workers are to be sent on their way in a manner worthy of God, so that the welcome reflects His own character. He values quiet, dependable service to strangers and counts it as real partnership in His mission, making the humble host a fellow worker for the truth.

crossReferences.note: Whoever welcomes Jesus' messengers welcomes Him, and even a cup of cold water to them is noticed and rewarded.
The Lord commanded that those who preach the gospel should receive their living from it.
Share with the saints who are in need and practise hospitality, the everyday duty modelled by Gaius.
Do not neglect hospitality to strangers, for some have entertained angels without knowing it.

study.oneStory: God's mission to the nations advances through those who are sent and those who send. Gaius's open door is one bright thread in that story: an ordinary believer sustaining travelling workers so the gospel can run freely, and in doing so becoming a fellow worker for the truth. The same partnership carries the gospel to the ends of the earth and toward the day when people from every nation are gathered home.

sermon.title: Walk in the Truth, Part Two
sermon.why: In this second message on 3 John, Begg treats Gaius's hospitality and generosity, showing how supporting gospel workers makes believers fellow workers for the truth. A faithful, practical exposition of this movement.

quiz.q: Why does supporting the travelling workers matter so much?

options.text: By supporting them, believers become fellow workers for the truth, sharing in the mission itself.
options.note: Verse 8 makes support a genuine partnership in the work of the gospel.

options.text: Because hospitality is a nice thing to do for guests.
options.note: It is kind, but the deeper point is real partnership in mission, not mere courtesy.

options.text: Because the workers would otherwise be lonely on the road.
options.note: Companionship helps, but the issue is sustaining the gospel mission.

options.text: Because it earns the giver a good reputation.
options.note: The motive is the Name of Christ and the truth, not personal reputation.

quiz.q: What is striking about the people Gaius served?

options.text: They were strangers to him, welcomed for Christ's sake rather than because they were friends.
options.note: Verse 5 stresses that he served brothers who were strangers, the heart of true hospitality.

options.text: They were fellow believers, the brothers, in genuine need of help.
options.note: True, but the striking detail is that they were strangers to him, not merely brothers.

options.text: They were local church members he already knew well.
options.note: They were travelling workers, strangers to Gaius, not familiar locals.

options.text: They were wealthy patrons who repaid him.
options.note: They took nothing from outsiders; they depended on the church's support.

quiz.q: Why did the workers accept nothing from the Gentiles?

options.text: So the gospel would be free and its support would rest on God's people.
options.note: Refusing pagan money kept the message free and placed the duty on the church, verses 7 and 8.

options.text: So they would not appear to be preaching the gospel for money.
options.note: A real part of it: guarding the gospel's credibility, though the elder's stress is that the church must support them.

options.text: Because they had taken a vow of poverty.
options.note: The text does not mention a vow; the concern is the gospel's credibility and the church's part.

options.text: Because Gentiles had no money to give.
options.note: It was a deliberate principle, not a lack of available funds.

quiz.q: What does sending them on their way worthy of God mean?

options.text: Providing generously and honourably, in a way that reflects the God they serve.
options.note: Worthy of God calls for open-handed, fitting support, verse 6.

options.text: Equipping them well with provisions for the next stage of the journey.
options.note: This captures the practical sense of send on their way, though worthy of God adds the honouring motive.

options.text: Giving them the bare minimum to get by.
options.note: The standard is worthy of God, which rules out grudging or minimal help.

options.text: Sending them away as quickly as possible.
options.note: The aim is generous support, the opposite of hurrying them off.

questions: Gaius welcomed and supported strangers for the sake of Christ. Who are the gospel workers in your life, and how could you support them more generously?
The elder says supporting such workers makes us fellow workers for the truth. Do you see your giving and hospitality as real partnership in mission, or as an optional extra?
True hospitality here means love of strangers, not just of friends. Where is God calling you to open your home, table, or resources to someone you do not yet know?
The workers refused to depend on outsiders so the gospel would stay free. How can you help keep the good news freely offered in your own setting?
This positive picture of Gaius stands right before the warning about Diotrephes. When it comes to welcoming others, which of the two are you more like this season?

translationNotes.note: The phrase for the brothers, and especially since they are strangers to you, highlights hospitality, philoxenia, which literally means love of strangers.
Send them on their way, propempo, was a near technical term for equipping travellers with provisions, money, and companions for the next stage of the journey.
The Name stands for Jesus; going out for the Name means for His sake. The Gentiles here are unbelieving outsiders from whom the workers would take no support.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 3 John 1:5-8.
